risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "study participants were blindly randomized into one of the following four groups" Comment: The article states that participants were randomized, but it does not describe the actual sequence-generation method (e.g., computer-generated list, random number table, block/stratified randomization). Under Cochrane guidance, "randomized" alone without further context is insufficient to confirm a truly random allocation sequence, but there is also no explicit non-random rule described.

_evidence.method_quote: Following the medication washout period, study participants were blindly randomized into one of the following four groups: (1) 10 mg fixed dose of haloperidol for 6 days, followed by a clinically determined optimal dose of haloperidol (10–20 mg) for 5 weeks; (2) 12.5 mg fixed dose of olanzapine for 6 days, followed by a clinically determined optimal dose of olanzapine (12.5–25 mg) for 5 weeks; (3) placebo for 6 days followed by a clinically determined optimal dose of haloperidol (10–20 mg) for 5 weeks; or (4) placebo for 6 days followed by a clinically determined optimal dose of olanzapine (12.5–25 mg) for 5 weeks.
_evidence.method_summary: Participants were blindly randomized into one of four treatment groups after medication washout.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "study participants were blindly randomized into one of the following four groups" Comment: The article states that participants were randomized and that medications were prepared in similar-looking capsules by the hospital pharmacist, but it does not explicitly describe any allocation concealment mechanism (e.g., central allocation, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, or identical numbered containers) that would prevent foreknowledge of the next assignment by the recruiter. Therefore allocation concealment cannot be confirmed and remains unclear.

_evidence.concealment_quote: study participants were blindly randomized into one of the following four groups
_evidence.note: Article describes blinded randomization and that medications were prepared in similar-looking capsules by the hospital pharmacist, but it does not describe the sequence generation or any envelope/central allocation details.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "study participants were blindly randomized into one of the following four groups" and "Medications were prepared in similar-looking capsules by the hospital pharmacist. Optimal dose was determined by treating psychiatrists, who blindly adjusted medication in pre-determined increments" Comment: The article explicitly states that participants were blinded and that treating psychiatrists also adjusted medication blindly, with identical-looking capsules used to maintain masking. Although one outcome (BPRS Psychosis Subscale score change) is subjective, successful blinding of both participants and personnel makes performance bias unlikely.

_evidence.blinding_quote: "study participants were blindly randomized into one of the following four groups" and "Medications were prepared in similar-looking capsules by the hospital pharmacist. Optimal dose was determined by treating psychiatrists, who blindly adjusted medication in pre-determined increments"

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants’ symptoms were evaluated using the BPRS at each session." Comment: The main subjective outcome here (BPRS symptom rating) was assessed, but the study states that participants were "blindly randomized" and that treating psychiatrists "blindly adjusted medication"; the outcome measurement itself is presented as a structured rating scale rather than an unblinded clinician impression, and the objective PET/task outcomes were measured by automated imaging/analysis. Because no evidence is given that the BPRS assessor was unblinded, and the report does not indicate an unblinded assessor for the subjective outcome, detection bias is not demonstrated.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: "Additionally, participants’ symptoms were evaluated using the BPRS at each session."

outcomes.label: task response accuracies and reaction times

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Thirty-seven patients initially provided informed consent; however, eight dropped out during the study period [details of participant drop out were reported in Lahti et al. (2009)]." Comment: The article confirms post-randomization dropout occurred, but it does not provide the per-arm randomized vs analysed numbers, the analysed denominator at the relevant time point(s), or the reasons for loss in this report. Because the attrition magnitude and balance across arms cannot be computed from the supplied text, there is insufficient evidence to judge this domain High or Low; per Cochrane guidance this is Unclear risk.

per_arm_flow.reasons: “Thirty-seven patients initially provided informed consent; however, eight dropped out during the study period [details of participant drop out were reported in Lahti et al. (2009)].”

_evidence.notes: The article states that 8 participants dropped out, but provides no per-arm attrition, no analysed n for the primary/secondary outcomes, and says dropout details were reported in another paper (Lahti et al. 2009). Tables 4–10 report analyses with varying degrees of freedom, but not the per-arm loss needed to compute attrition.
